To make out an offence of possessing drugs for the purpose of trafficking, the Prosecution must prove that (Chong Hoon Cheong v Public Prosecutor [2022] 2 SLR 778 (“Chong Hoon Cheong”) at [4]): (a) the accused person possessed the controlled drug in question (“Possession Element”); (b) the accused person knew the nature of the drug (“Knowledge Element”); and (c) such possession of the drug was for the purpose of trafficking which was not authorised (“Purpose Element”).

For the two charges against the accused to be made out, three elements had to be established (Masoud Rahimi bin Mehrzad v Public Prosecutor [2017] 1 SLR 257 at [28]): (a) the accused was in possession of the drugs; (b) the accused had knowledge of the drugs; and (c) the accused possessed the drugs for the purpose of trafficking.

The appellant, Mr Abdullah bin Mohammad Kunhi, appealed against his conviction and sentence in respect of a charge of attempted possession of not less than 15.01g of methylenedioxymethamphetamine or “MDMA”, which has a street name of “ecstasy”, for the purpose of trafficking (the “Attempted Trafficking Charge”). Accordingly, I was satisfied that the Prosecution had proven beyond a reasonable doubt that the accused possessed the drugs for the purpose of trafficking. This was sufficient for me to convict the accused of the First Charge and Second Charge. The appellant claims that Liberty is liable to him under s 9(1); it is for him to prove that the requirements under s 9(1) have been met so that liability may be imposed on Liberty. One of these requirements is that Stephen was a person insured by the Policy.

A large number of divorces commenced each year at the Family Justice Courts are filed on the “simplified track”, without parties contesting the divorce and ancillary matters. Such harmonious resolution of issues reached by the agreement of parties is aligned with the endeavours of a therapeutic justice system, which aims to assist parties to move forward without taking adversarial stances against each other.

The District Judge of the Family Court (the “DJ”) ordered that (a) the matrimonial home be sold immediately, (b) parties were to bear the mortgage loan repayments and property tax for it equally from the date of his order on 20 October 2023, and (c) the Wife’s CPF refund was to be made from her own share of the net proceeds. The DJ declined to make any order on the payment of living expenses or for repairs and maintenance of the matrimonial home; he also dismissed the Wife’s application in SUM 1

advanced_b_hybrid_rerank_paragraph.generated_answer: The court declined to allow the $12,020 claim for reinstatement works because it was not clear whether the sums claimed were specifically for the purposes of reinstatement or renovations/improvements for the defendant's benefit. Additionally, the defendant did not provide evidence from contractors/vendors to support the claim, and special damages must be specifically pleaded and proven. Therefore, the court was not satisfied that the defendant had proven the $12,020 on a balance of probabilities.

advanced_b_hybrid_rerank_paragraph.question: Why was the $5,600 claim for loss of use rejected?
advanced_b_hybrid_rerank_paragraph.reference_answer: There was insufficient evidence linking the claimed period to reinstatement work.
advanced_b_hybrid_rerank_paragraph.generated_answer:
The defendant's claim for $5,600 for loss of use of the premises for one month was rejected because the evidence from the contractors/vendors was not provided to support the claim that the work was specifically for the purpose of reinstatement of the premises.
